body_txt: Bullies . We all know them, we all hate them, and one big stereotype is that they're one-dimensional jerkasses who may commit some of the most vicious acts of cruelty towards their victims , sometimes just for sheer sadism . But not every bully is like this. Being an asshole doesn't mean you're evil , and many will draw the line somewhere . And in many cases, the audience will get a good look at exactly what sort of lines a bully is and isn't willing to cross. This can happen in many different ways, including, but not limited to:  The main rules here are simply that the line should be a moral one (in that they recognize that certain things are wrong) and that the act should be played as a humanizing moment even if they're otherwise nothing but cruel to those around them. Their standards don't necessarily have to make sense on a scale of what we the audience consider worse; it's about personal morals, which may reflect normal standards less than the bully's personal view of reality. Still, however it manifests, it becomes clear that the bully in question does have a good side to them, even if it's hidden, and they won't tolerate acts that go beyond what they consider an "acceptable" way to treat the victim. Both the audience and their victim will see that they aren't completely cruel. The lines they refuse to cross show where their personal values lie, as well as what they cannot justify even to themselves. Usually this involves directly showing compassion to one of their victims, most often the protagonist, who is likely the main target of their cruelty. In other cases, though, it'll involve someone besides their usual victim. As long as it's someone or something they'd be expected to target (rather than, say, a member of their clique who is usually treated with kindness), it makes it clear that they find certain things off-limits and wouldn't condone certain things happening to anyone , whether it's their usual punching bag or some kid they just met. One easy way to make this happen is for the bully to be contrasted against someone even worse ; we can judge from their reactions, whether they're visibly uncomfortable or make the effort to stop it, and it has the added benefit of characterizing the other bully as particularly nasty. This particular tactic works well to make individual members of a Gang of Bullies , Girl Posse , or the Beta Bitch more sympathetic than the meaner ringleader. A common variant is for the bully to stand up in defense of their usual victim when their victim is getting the same treatment from anybody else: "Nobody picks on them except for me!" A true Hypocritical Heartwarming moment. The impact of the moment will vary. In some works, this moment will be a catalyst for the bully reforming , becoming a friend of the hero , or both. Such works usually have this as a major Humanizing Moment that alters the portrayal of the bully going forward, or occasionally will even change the direction of the story itself. In others, though, the status quo remains under the premise that the bully has always had this standard and still chose to be a bully; they may very well return to their nasty ways as soon as the moment passes. In this case, the victim may never forgive their bully, or may never learn about their "good side" at all. Subtrope of Everyone Has Standards . Compare Even Evil Has Standards , where a villain has a line they won't cross; in this case, bullies aren't necessarily 'evil', and this trope ensures they don't slide too far down, as well as Unexpected Kindness and Enemy Compassion , which can often overlap here. Contrast Bully Brutality , for bullies who are willing to seriously injure or even kill their victims, and Save the Jerk , for where a victim may save their bully from danger. Compare and contrast Abuse Escalation ; while most of these bullies have a very clear line they'll stop at, some will continue escalating things even if they have standards. Also Compare and contrast Self-Righteous Bully , for bullies who think of themselves as the good guy regardless of whether or not they have standards.
